/plan.description: Respond with a structured plan only, no code changes
/plan.task_prefix: IMPORTANT: You are in PLANNING MODE. Analyze the codebase and produce a structured implementation plan. Do NOT modify any files or write any code. Structure your plan as: 1) Current state analysis, 2) Proposed changes (files + approach), 3) Risks and tradeoffs, 4) Testing strategy. Post your plan as a comment on the issue.

/review.description: Review your own open PR and post findings
/review.task_prefix: Review your open PR for this issue. Read the full diff and check for: hardcoded values, missing error handling, test coverage gaps, security issues, and code style. Post your findings as a comment. Do NOT modify any code.

/explain.description: Explain a file, concept, or area of the codebase
/explain.task_prefix: Explain the requested topic. Provide a clear, thorough explanation with references to specific files and line numbers where relevant. Do NOT modify any files.

/test.description: Run tests and report results without modifying code
/test.task_prefix: Run the test suite and report results. If tests fail, analyze the failures and explain likely causes. Do NOT modify any code or test files.

/search.description: Research a topic using web search, no code changes
/search.task_prefix: You are in RESEARCH MODE. Use web search and web fetch to research the requested topic. Provide a thorough answer with sources and references. Do NOT modify any files or write any code. Post your findings as a comment on the issue.

/security.description: Security audit — check for vulnerabilities in code
/security.task_prefix:
You are a senior security engineer conducting a focused security review. Analyze the codebase for HIGH-CONFIDENCE security vulnerabilities with real exploitation potential. Focus ONLY on security implications, not general code quality.

SECURITY CATEGORIES TO EXAMINE:
- Input Validation: SQL injection, command injection, path traversal, XXE, template injection
- Authentication & Authorization: bypass logic, privilege escalation, session flaws, JWT vulnerabilities
- Crypto & Secrets: hardcoded keys/passwords, weak algorithms, improper key storage
- Injection & Code Execution: deserialization, eval injection, XSS (reflected, stored, DOM-based)
- Data Exposure: sensitive data logging, PII handling, API data leakage, debug info exposure

METHODOLOGY:
1. Repository context — identify existing security frameworks, patterns, and threat model
2. Comparative analysis — compare changes against established secure practices
3. Vulnerability assessment — trace data flow from inputs to sensitive operations, identify injection points

SEVERITY GUIDELINES:
- HIGH: Directly exploitable — RCE, data breach, auth bypass
- MEDIUM: Requires specific conditions but significant impact
- LOW: Defense-in-depth issues

Only flag issues where you are >80% confident of actual exploitability. Skip theoretical issues, style concerns, DoS, secrets-on-disk, rate limiting, or low-impact findings. For each finding, provide: file, line number, severity, category, description, exploit scenario, and fix recommendation.

Post your findings as a comment on the issue. Do NOT modify any code.

/fix.description: Diagnose and fix a specific bug or error
/fix.task_prefix:
BUGFIX MODE. Diagnose the reported bug or error systematically:
1) Reproduce or locate the issue — find the relevant code and understand the failure
2) Identify root cause — trace the execution path, check inputs/outputs, examine error messages
3) Apply a minimal, targeted fix — change only what is necessary to resolve the issue
4) Verify the fix — run existing tests if available, confirm the fix addresses the root cause
5) Post a summary of what you found and what you changed as a comment on the issue

Do NOT refactor surrounding code or make unrelated improvements. Keep the fix focused and minimal.

/refactor.description: Improve code quality without changing behavior
/refactor.task_prefix:
REFACTORING MODE. Improve code quality while preserving existing behavior:
1) Analyze the target code for: code smells (long methods, duplication, magic numbers, poor naming), SOLID violations, and unnecessary complexity
2) Prioritize improvements: start with high-impact/low-effort changes (extract constants, rename for clarity), then method extraction and decomposition
3) Apply refactoring — ensure all changes preserve the existing behavior. Do NOT add new features or change functionality
4) Verify: run existing tests if available. If tests break, the refactoring introduced a bug — fix it
5) Post a summary of changes with before/after comparison as a comment on the issue

/deps.description: Audit dependencies for vulnerabilities and outdated packages
/deps.task_prefix:
DEPENDENCY AUDIT MODE. Analyze project dependencies for security and maintenance risks:
1) Identify all dependency files (package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod, Cargo.toml, etc.)
2) Check for known vulnerabilities — run available audit tools (npm audit, pip-audit, cargo audit, etc.)
3) Identify outdated packages — list packages with available updates, noting major vs minor version bumps
4) Flag high-risk dependencies — unmaintained packages, packages with known CVEs, packages with excessive transitive dependencies
5) Provide actionable remediation: which packages to update, which to replace, and priority order

Post your findings as a structured report in a comment on the issue. Do NOT modify any files.

ci_commands._comment: These commands are handled by 'sandbox ci-watch' on the host, not the agent. Post them as PR comments. Defined in ci-commands.json.

/test-pr-bug.description: Verify a bug fix: test must FAIL on base branch, PASS on PR branch

/test-pr.description: Run a test command on a branch in a clean container
